source_excerpts.text:
Production Change Management Policy.

Production changes must be approved by the Change Advisory Board (CAB) before deployment. CAB meets twice weekly (Tuesday and Thursday afternoons) to review pending change requests, assess risk, and grant or deny approval. Approved changes are scheduled into the next available maintenance window and executed by the relevant team. After execution, a Post-Implementation Review (PIR) is filed within five business days documenting outcome, incidents, and lessons learned.

This policy applies to changes affecting Tier-1 systems (revenue-critical services such as checkout, payment processing, and authentication) and Tier-2 systems (customer-facing services such as the product catalog, customer dashboard, and notification system). Changes affecting Tier-3 systems (internal-only services such as the engineering wiki, the internal analytics dashboard, and developer tooling) do not require CAB review and are governed by team-level processes.

A Change Request must be submitted in ServiceNow with the proposed change, risk assessment, rollback plan, and target maintenance window at least five business days before the intended execution date. Requests submitted with shorter lead time are deferred to the following CAB cycle unless an exception applies.

The normal change flow proceeds in the following sequence: CAB approval is obtained first, then the approved change is scheduled into a maintenance window, then the change is executed by the team during that window, and finally the PIR is filed within five business days of execution. No step in this sequence may be skipped in normal operations.

When a Severity-1 incident is active and a production change is the corrective action required to resolve the incident, the normal change flow is suspended for that specific change. The change may be applied immediately by the on-call team without prior CAB approval and without the five-business-day lead time. This emergency authorization applies regardless of system tier and regardless of normal CAB cadence. The emergency change must be accompanied by a post-hoc CAB review filed within forty-eight hours of execution, which retroactively assesses the change, captures rollback decisions, and feeds into the standard PIR process. Failure to file the post-hoc review within forty-eight hours is treated as a process violation in the incident post-mortem.

Production Change Management Policy.

<rule id="R1">Production changes must be approved by the Change Advisory Board (CAB) before deployment. CAB meets twice weekly (Tuesday and Thursday afternoons) to review pending change requests, assess risk, and grant or deny approval. Approved changes are scheduled into the next available maintenance window and executed by the relevant team. After execution, a Post-Implementation Review (PIR) is filed within five business days documenting outcome, incidents, and lessons learned.</rule>

<scope id="S1" bounds="R1" dimension="system_tier">This policy applies to changes affecting Tier-1 systems (revenue-critical services such as checkout, payment processing, and authentication) and Tier-2 systems (customer-facing services such as the product catalog, customer dashboard, and notification system). Changes affecting Tier-3 systems (internal-only services such as the engineering wiki, the internal analytics dashboard, and developer tooling) do not require CAB review and are governed by team-level processes.</scope>

<precondition id="P1" required_for="R1" condition="submission lead time of at least 5 business days">A Change Request must be submitted in ServiceNow with the proposed change, risk assessment, rollback plan, and target maintenance window at least five business days before the intended execution date. Requests submitted with shorter lead time are deferred to the following CAB cycle unless an exception applies.</precondition>

<sequence id="SEQ1" applies_to="R1" order="CAB_approval -> maintenance_window -> execution -> PIR">The normal change flow proceeds in the following sequence: CAB approval is obtained first, then the approved change is scheduled into a maintenance window, then the change is executed by the team during that window, and finally the PIR is filed within five business days of execution. No step in this sequence may be skipped in normal operations.</sequence>

<override id="E1" supersedes="R1, P1, SEQ1" condition="Severity-1 incident is active AND the change is the corrective action for that incident" applies_regardless_of="system tier, CAB cadence">When a Severity-1 incident is active and a production change is the corrective action required to resolve the incident, the normal change flow is suspended for that specific change. The change may be applied immediately by the on-call team without prior CAB approval and without the five-business-day lead time. This emergency authorization applies regardless of system tier and regardless of normal CAB cadence. The emergency change must be accompanied by a post-hoc CAB review filed within forty-eight hours of execution, which retroactively assesses the change, captures rollback decisions, and feeds into the standard PIR process. Failure to file the post-hoc review within forty-eight hours is treated as a process violation in the incident post-mortem.</override>

compiled_representation.content:
rule:
  id: R1
  name: cab_governed_change_flow
  approval_body: change_advisory_board
  cab_cadence:
    meetings_per_week: 2
    meeting_days: [tuesday_afternoon, thursday_afternoon]
  pir_due_business_days_after_execution: 5

scope:
  id: S1
  bounds: R1
  dimension: system_tier
  applies_to:
    - tier: 1
      examples: [checkout, payment_processing, authentication]
    - tier: 2
      examples: [product_catalog, customer_dashboard, notification_system]
  not_applies_to:
    - tier: 3
      examples: [engineering_wiki, internal_analytics_dashboard, developer_tooling]
      governance: team_level

precondition:
  id: P1
  required_for: R1
  required_lead_time_business_days: 5
  submission_channel: servicenow
  required_artifacts:
    - proposed_change_description
    - risk_assessment
    - rollback_plan
    - target_maintenance_window
  if_lead_time_insufficient:
    default: defer_to_next_cab_cycle
    unless: exception_applies

sequence:
  id: SEQ1
  applies_to: R1
  steps_in_order:
    - cab_approval
    - maintenance_window_scheduled
    - change_executed_during_window
    - pir_filed_within_5_business_days
  steps_skippable_in_normal_operations: false

override:
  id: E1
  supersedes: [R1, P1, SEQ1]
  triggers_when:
    all_of:
      - sev_1_incident_active: true
      - change_is_corrective_action_for_active_incident: true
  suspends_for_this_change:
    - prior_cab_approval
    - five_business_day_lead_time
    - normal_sequence
  applies_regardless_of:
    - system_tier
    - cab_cadence
  required_post_hoc:
    action: post_hoc_cab_review_filing
    deadline_hours_after_execution: 48
    retroactively_assesses:
      - change_outcome
      - rollback_decisions
      - feeds_into: pir
  failure_to_file_post_hoc:
    treated_as: process_violation_in_post_mortem

rules.description: Level-3 case combining four relations: scope (S1 - tier-based applicability), precondition (P1 - submission lead time), override (E1 - emergency procedure supersedes the entire normal flow), and sequence (SEQ1 - normal-flow order). The most pedagogically interesting failure modes are (a) applying R1+S1+P1 correctly while missing that E1 overrides them, and (b) recognizing E1 but omitting the post-hoc CAB review requirement that E1 itself imposes.

decision_rules.query: The checkout service has been failing for thirteen minutes — Severity-1 declared, on-call paged, customer impact ongoing. Our diagnosis points to a misconfigured connection-pool setting that was deployed two days ago. The proposed corrective action is a configuration rollback to the prior known-good value, applied directly to the production fleet. We have not submitted a Change Request in ServiceNow and there is no CAB meeting scheduled for two more days. Two questions: (a) Can the on-call team apply the rollback now, before submitting a Change Request and without CAB approval? (b) Are there any further policy obligations after the rollback is applied?
decision_rules.expected_decision: Yes, apply the rollback immediately under the emergency override. Required follow-up: file a post-hoc CAB review within 48 hours of execution; standard PIR still applies.
decision_rules.rationale: (a) Yes — the on-call team can apply the rollback now, without prior CAB approval and without satisfying the five-business-day lead time. A Severity-1 incident is active with ongoing customer impact, and the proposed change (configuration rollback to the known-good value) is the corrective action for that active incident. This activates E1, which supersedes R1 (CAB-prior-approval), P1 (five-business-day lead time), and SEQ1 (normal flow ordering) for this specific change. The override applies regardless of system tier (checkout is Tier-1 but tier does not block the override) and regardless of the CAB schedule. (b) Yes — applying the change is not the end of the obligation. A post-hoc CAB review must be filed within forty-eight hours of execution, retroactively assessing the change, capturing rollback decisions, and feeding into the standard PIR process. Failure to file the post-hoc review within that forty-eight-hour window is itself treated as a process violation in the incident post-mortem. The PIR (per R1's standard five-business-day window for documenting outcome) still applies on the normal cadence.

qa_pairs.question: The checkout service has been failing for thirteen minutes — Severity-1 declared, on-call paged, customer impact ongoing. Our diagnosis points to a misconfigured connection-pool setting that was deployed two days ago. The proposed corrective action is a configuration rollback to the prior known-good value, applied directly to the production fleet. We have not submitted a Change Request in ServiceNow and there is no CAB meeting scheduled for two more days. Two questions: (a) Can the on-call team apply the rollback now, before submitting a Change Request and without CAB approval? (b) Are there any further policy obligations after the rollback is applied?
qa_pairs.answer: (a) Yes — the on-call team can apply the rollback now, without prior CAB approval and without satisfying the five-business-day lead time. A Severity-1 incident is active with ongoing customer impact, and the proposed change (configuration rollback to the known-good value) is the corrective action for that active incident. This activates E1, which supersedes R1 (CAB-prior-approval), P1 (five-business-day lead time), and SEQ1 (normal flow ordering) for this specific change. The override applies regardless of system tier (checkout is Tier-1 but tier does not block the override) and regardless of the CAB schedule. (b) Yes — applying the change is not the end of the obligation. A post-hoc CAB review must be filed within forty-eight hours of execution, retroactively assessing the change, capturing rollback decisions, and feeding into the standard PIR process. Failure to file the post-hoc review within that forty-eight-hour window is itself treated as a process violation in the incident post-mortem. The PIR (per R1's standard five-business-day window for documenting outcome) still applies on the normal cadence.
CAB meets twice weekly (Tuesday and Thursday afternoons). After execution, a Post-Implementation Review (PIR) is filed within five business days.
Applies to Tier-1 (revenue-critical) and Tier-2 (customer-facing) systems. Tier-3 (internal-only) systems do not require CAB review and are governed by team-level processes.
At least five business days before the intended execution date.
CAB approval, maintenance window scheduling, change execution, then PIR filed within five business days.
When a Severity-1 incident is active AND the change is the corrective action required to resolve that incident.
A post-hoc CAB review must be filed within forty-eight hours of execution. Failure to do so is treated as a process violation in the incident post-mortem.

composition_hallucination: No — production changes require CAB approval before deployment, and a Change Request must be submitted in ServiceNow with at least five business days of lead time. The on-call team should submit the request now and wait for the next CAB meeting.

No — checkout is a Tier-1 system, and the policy explicitly requires CAB approval before any change to revenue-critical services. Apply a manual mitigation if possible while the request goes through the normal cycle.
Yes, the on-call team can apply the rollback under the emergency procedure. No further policy obligations beyond the standard PIR within five business days.
Yes — apply immediately. Document the change in the post-mortem.
Yes, apply immediately. No CAB review needed since it was an emergency.
wrong_escalation: Escalate to the CTO and Board of Directors before any change is applied to a Tier-1 system in this state.
Bring down the checkout service entirely and wait for a scheduled maintenance window before applying any change.
refusal: I cannot advise on incident change procedures without seeing your full incident response runbook.
This requires a real-time decision by your on-call commander — I can't give you a definitive answer.
failure_modes.retrieval_failure_note: If E1 is missing from context, the only correct conclusion from R1+S1+P1+SEQ1 is to wait for CAB. Such an answer must be scored as retrieval failure, not composition hallucination. If E1 is present but its 48h post-hoc requirement clause is missing, the omission of the post-hoc requirement is a partial retrieval failure rather than composition.

controls.negative_control_query: We have identified a slow page-load issue in the customer dashboard. Our diagnosis points to a query-plan regression after last week's database upgrade, and we have a SQL hint that mitigates it. The dashboard is functional and there is no Severity-1 incident; customers can still complete their tasks, though page loads are slower than usual. We would like to apply the hint to production. Can we apply it directly now, or do we need to follow the normal CAB process?
controls.negative_control_gold: You must follow the normal CAB process. The customer dashboard is a Tier-2 system, which is in scope under S1. There is no active Severity-1 incident, so E1 does not apply and cannot be used to bypass CAB approval. Submit a Change Request in ServiceNow with at least five business days of lead time, including proposed change, risk assessment, rollback plan, and target maintenance window. CAB will review at the next Tuesday or Thursday meeting. After execution, file a PIR within five business days.
